Title: Cold Email That Gets 20%+ Replies (Under 90 Words)
Teaser: Writes a tight, personalized cold email with a scroll-stopping subject line and one low-friction ask that beats spam filters and ghosting.
PromptHint: Fill [PROSPECT/COMPANY], [THEIR LIKELY PAIN], your [OFFER], and one [SPECIFIC TRIGGER] you noticed.
Prompt:
You are a top SDR whose cold emails get 20%+ reply rates. Write a cold email to [PROSPECT/COMPANY].

THEIR LIKELY PAIN: [THEIR LIKELY PAIN]
MY OFFER: [OFFER]
SPECIFIC TRIGGER I NOTICED (news, post, hire, launch): [SPECIFIC TRIGGER]

Think first (don't show this): what does this person care about this week, and what's the smallest yes I can ask for?

Rules:
- Open with the trigger, not me. Prove I did my homework in line 1.
- Body under 90 words, 5th-grade reading level, no jargon.
- One clear value tied to their pain. No feature dumps.
- End with ONE low-friction ask (e.g. 'worth a 10-min look?'), never 'hop on a call'.
- Banned: 'I hope this finds you well', 'circling back', 'synergy', 'revolutionary', exclamation marks.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 options, under 40 chars, no spam-trigger words
2) EMAIL BODY
3) PS — one line
4) SPAM CHECK — flag any phrase a filter might catch, or write 'clean'

Title: The 4-Touch Follow-Up Sequence That Doesn't Annoy
Teaser: Builds a polite-but-persistent follow-up series that adds new value each time instead of 'just bumping this' — turning silence into replies.
PromptHint: Paste your [ORIGINAL EMAIL] and describe the [DEAL/ASK] and [PROSPECT TYPE].
Prompt:
You are a sales follow-up strategist. Build a 4-email follow-up sequence for someone who went silent after my first message.

ORIGINAL EMAIL I SENT:
[ORIGINAL EMAIL]
WHAT I'M ASKING FOR: [DEAL/ASK]
PROSPECT TYPE: [PROSPECT TYPE]

Rules:
- Each email must add NEW value (a resource, a relevant result, a reframed angle) — never just 'following up' or 'bumping this'.
- Vary the angle: Touch 1 = helpful nudge, Touch 2 = social proof / result, Touch 3 = different pain angle, Touch 4 = graceful breakup that invites a reply.
- Each under 70 words, friendly and low-pressure, never guilt-trippy.
- Suggest send timing (days apart) for each.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A table: | Touch | Day | Subject | Body | Why it works |
- Then one line: the single psychological reason the breakup email (Touch 4) so often gets the reply.

Title: Hard Conversation Script: Say It Without Burning the Bridge
Teaser: Turns a dreaded message — pushback, a 'no', a boundary, bad news — into a clear, kind, firm email that protects the relationship.
PromptHint: Describe the [SITUATION], who it's [TO], the [OUTCOME YOU WANT], and the [RELATIONSHIP] (boss, client, peer).
Prompt:
You are a communication coach trained in nonviolent communication and executive feedback. Help me write a hard email I've been avoiding.

SITUATION: [SITUATION]
WHO IT'S TO: [TO]
OUTCOME I WANT: [OUTCOME YOU WANT]
OUR RELATIONSHIP: [RELATIONSHIP]

Do this:
1. Name the one thing I'm really afraid will happen if I send this.
2. Write the email: lead with shared context/goodwill, state the issue using observation + impact (not blame or 'you always'), make ONE clear ask, and leave them a dignified path forward.
3. Keep it under 160 words, warm but firm — no over-apologizing, no passive aggression, no walls of text.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE EMAIL
(b) SOFTER VERSION and FIRMER VERSION
(c) ONE SENTENCE TO DELETE if I want to sound less defensive

Title: The Sincere Apology Email (No 'Sorry If', No Excuses)
Teaser: Writes an apology that actually repairs trust — owns the impact, skips the excuses, and proposes a concrete fix instead of a hollow 'sorry'.
PromptHint: Explain [WHAT HAPPENED], the [IMPACT ON THEM], who it's [TO], and what you'll [DO TO FIX IT].
Prompt:
You are an expert in trust repair and crisis communication. Write a genuine apology email.

WHAT HAPPENED: [WHAT HAPPENED]
IMPACT ON THEM: [IMPACT ON THEM]
WHO IT'S TO: [TO]
HOW I'LL FIX IT: [DO TO FIX IT]

Non-negotiable rules:
- Name the specific mistake plainly. No 'I'm sorry IF', 'mistakes were made', or hiding behind passive voice.
- Acknowledge the real impact on them before talking about me.
- Take full ownership — zero excuses, zero blaming circumstances or other people.
- State exactly what I'll do so it doesn't recur, with a timeframe.
- Don't grovel or over-apologize; one clean apology, not five.
- Under 150 words, human and direct.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) SUBJECT LINE — one line that signals accountability without panic
(b) THE APOLOGY EMAIL
(c) 3 PHRASES TO NEVER USE here, each with one reason why

Title: Salary Negotiation Email: Counter Without Killing the Deal
Teaser: Drafts a confident counter-offer email that anchors high, justifies the ask with value, and keeps the tone collaborative so they say yes.
PromptHint: Add the [CURRENT OFFER], your [TARGET], your [TOP 2 LEVERAGE POINTS], and what you're [NEGOTIATING].
Prompt:
You are a negotiation coach (Chris Voss / Harvard 'Getting to Yes' style). Write a counter-offer email.

WHAT I'M NEGOTIATING: [NEGOTIATING]
CURRENT OFFER: [CURRENT OFFER]
MY TARGET: [TARGET]
MY TOP 2 LEVERAGE POINTS: [TOP 2 LEVERAGE POINTS]

Rules:
- Open with genuine enthusiasm for the role so it never reads as ungrateful.
- Anchor with a specific number/ask backed by value and market reality, not need.
- Use collaborative framing ('how can we get to X together?') not ultimatums.
- Keep one specific, reasonable ask front and center; bundle minor asks below.
- Stay under 180 words, calm and confident — no apologizing for asking.

This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE EMAIL
(b) FALLBACK LINE if they say the number is fixed
(c) THE ONE PHRASE that signals I'm serious but flexible

Title: Meeting Notes → Decisions, Owners & Action Items
Teaser: Turns a messy meeting transcript or notes into a crisp recap with an action-item table (owner + deadline) you can send in 30 seconds.
PromptHint: Paste the [TRANSCRIPT/NOTES] and name the [MEETING PURPOSE] so it sorts what matters.
Prompt:
You are an executive chief-of-staff who writes flawless meeting recaps. Turn the notes below into a send-ready summary.

MEETING PURPOSE: [MEETING PURPOSE]
TRANSCRIPT / NOTES:
[TRANSCRIPT/NOTES]

Rules: only use what's in the notes, never invent commitments, keep it scannable. If an owner or date was never stated, write 'UNASSIGNED — confirm' rather than guessing.

OUTPUT FORMAT (this exact order):
1. TL;DR — 2-3 sentences a busy exec reads in 10 seconds
2. DECISIONS MADE — bullets of what was actually decided (not just discussed)
3. ACTION ITEMS — a table: | Action | Owner | Deadline | Status |
4. OPEN QUESTIONS / BLOCKERS — what still needs an answer and from whom
5. NEXT MEETING — date/topic if mentioned
6. EMAIL INTRO — one copy-paste line to send this recap to attendees

Title: Inbox Triage: Clear My Backlog With Drafts in My Voice
Teaser: Reads a pile of unanswered emails, sorts them by urgency, and drafts a reply to each in your tone so you clear the backlog in minutes.
PromptHint: Paste the [EMAILS] (or thread dump) and describe your [TONE] + any [DEFAULT STANCE] (e.g. protect my time).
Prompt:
You are my executive assistant. I'll paste several unanswered emails; help me clear them fast in MY voice.

MY TONE: [TONE]
MY DEFAULT STANCE (e.g. protect my calendar, say no kindly): [DEFAULT STANCE]
EMAILS:
[EMAILS]

For each email:
1. Classify: REPLY NOW / QUICK YES / POLITE NO / NEEDS INFO / IGNORE — with a one-line reason.
2. For everything except IGNORE, draft a reply in my tone: under 80 words, one clear next step, no filler.
3. Where I'd need to decide something, leave a [YOU DECIDE: option A / option B] marker instead of assuming.

Don't send anything — these are drafts for me to approve.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- A numbered list, one block per email (classification + reason + draft)
- A final line: 'Top 3 to handle first' in priority order

Title: De-Robotify My Email: Kill the AI Tells, Sound Human
Teaser: Rewrites stiff, corporate, or obviously-AI email copy into something warm and natural — keeping your meaning but killing the tells.
PromptHint: Paste the [DRAFT EMAIL] and pick a [VOICE] (e.g. warm, direct, casual-professional).
Prompt:
You are an editor who makes business writing sound like a real, likeable human. Rewrite my email so no one can tell AI touched it.

DESIRED VOICE: [VOICE]
DRAFT EMAIL:
[DRAFT EMAIL]

Rules:
- Keep the meaning, key facts, and any names/numbers exactly.
- Cut corporate filler and AI tells: 'I hope this email finds you well', 'I wanted to reach out', 'delve', 'leverage', 'in today's fast-paced world', 'furthermore', overused em-dashes, and tidy rule-of-three lists.
- Use natural rhythm: vary sentence length, use contractions, allow the occasional short line.
- Stay concise; end with one clear ask.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE REWRITTEN EMAIL
(b) HUMANNESS CHECK — the 3 biggest AI tells you removed
(c) ONE OPTIONAL LINE that adds a touch of genuine personality

Title: Rewrite My Email: Clear, Under 150 Words, One Real Ask
Teaser: Takes a rambling, buried-the-lede email and rebuilds it so the reader knows what you want in the first two lines — the viral clarity template.
PromptHint: Paste the [MESSY EMAIL] and state the [ONE THING YOU ACTUALLY WANT] from the reader.
Prompt:
You are a ruthless editor obsessed with clarity. Rewrite my email so a busy person gets it on the first read.

THE ONE THING I ACTUALLY WANT: [ONE THING YOU ACTUALLY WANT]
MESSY EMAIL:
[MESSY EMAIL]

Rules:
- Put the bottom line up front: the ask must be obvious within the first 2 lines.
- Under 150 words total. Cut every sentence that doesn't serve the ask.
- If there are multiple points, use short labeled bullets.
- Make the call to action specific (what, who, by when). No vague 'let me know'.
- Preserve essential facts; remove hedging, apologies for length, and backstory the reader doesn't need.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) SUBJECT LINE
(b) THE REWRITTEN EMAIL
(c) ONE-LINE NOTE on the biggest thing that was burying my point

Title: Long Email Thread → What's Going On & What I Owe
Teaser: Collapses a 30-message thread into context, decisions, open loops, and exactly what's being asked of you — so you reply without re-reading everything.
PromptHint: Paste the full [EMAIL THREAD] and say [WHO YOU ARE] in it (e.g. the vendor, the manager).
Prompt:
You are a sharp analyst. I'll paste a long, tangled email thread. Tell me what actually happened and what I owe.

WHO I AM IN THIS THREAD: [WHO YOU ARE]
THREAD:
[EMAIL THREAD]

Rules: stick to the thread, flag anything ambiguous as 'unclear — confirm', don't invent obligations.

OUTPUT FORMAT (this order):
1. CONTEXT — 2-3 sentences: what this thread is about and where it stands
2. KEY DECISIONS / AGREEMENTS — bullets of what's been settled
3. OPEN LOOPS — unresolved questions and who they're waiting on
4. WHAT'S NEEDED FROM ME — a checklist of specific actions/replies I owe, with any stated deadlines
5. WATCH-OUTS — any tension, contradiction, or commitment I might miss
6. DRAFT REPLY — 2-3 sentences that close my biggest open loop

Title: Warm Intro Request People Actually Say Yes To
Teaser: Writes a respectful, easy-to-forward intro request (or cold outreach to someone you admire) that makes saying yes effortless.
PromptHint: Name [WHO YOU WANT TO REACH], [WHY], your [CONNECTION/ANGLE], and the [ONE SPECIFIC ASK].
Prompt:
You are a relationship-building expert who gets high reply rates from busy people. Write an outreach email.

WHO I WANT TO REACH: [WHO YOU WANT TO REACH]
WHY I'M REACHING OUT: [WHY]
MY CONNECTION OR ANGLE (mutual contact, their work, shared interest): [CONNECTION/ANGLE]
MY ONE SPECIFIC ASK: [ONE SPECIFIC ASK]

Rules:
- Lead with something genuinely specific about them — prove it's not a mass blast.
- Give before you take: offer value, context, or a reason I'm worth their time.
- Make the ask tiny and clear (one question, a 15-min chat, or a yes/no) — easy to grant.
- Respect their time explicitly; give them an easy out.
- Under 120 words, no flattery that sounds fake.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE EMAIL
(b) FORWARDABLE BLURB — a short paragraph the mutual contact can paste in one click (skip if not an intro request)
(c) 2 SUBJECT-LINE OPTIONS

Title: Customer Complaint Reply: Defuse, Own It, Win Them Back
Teaser: Crafts a calm, empathetic response to an angry customer or review that de-escalates, takes responsibility, and offers a concrete resolution.
PromptHint: Paste the [COMPLAINT], your [BRAND TONE], and what you can [OFFER] to make it right.
Prompt:
You are a head of customer experience known for turning angry customers into fans. Write a reply to this complaint.

THE COMPLAINT:
[COMPLAINT]
MY BRAND TONE: [BRAND TONE]
WHAT I CAN OFFER TO RESOLVE IT: [OFFER]

Rules:
- Open by acknowledging their feeling and the specific problem — no defensive 'we're sorry you feel that way'.
- Take ownership where we got it wrong; don't argue or make them prove it.
- State the concrete resolution and a clear timeline.
- Stay calm and human even if their message was harsh; never match the heat.
- Keep it under 140 words. Don't over-promise things I can't deliver.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE REPLY
(b) PUBLIC-REVIEW VERSION — a shorter version if this is posted in the open
(c) DEFUSE/INFLAME — the single line most likely to defuse their anger, and the one line to avoid that would inflame it

Title: The Graceful 'No' Email That Protects the Relationship
Teaser: Drafts a graceful decline — to a meeting, request, scope creep, or invite — that's firm and kind so you protect your time without guilt.
PromptHint: Describe [WHAT YOU'RE DECLINING], who it's [TO], and any [ALTERNATIVE] you can offer (or none).
Prompt:
You are a coach who helps people set boundaries without burning bridges. Write an email that says no, gracefully.

WHAT I'M DECLINING: [WHAT YOU'RE DECLINING]
WHO IT'S TO: [TO]
ALTERNATIVE I CAN OFFER (or 'none'): [ALTERNATIVE]

Rules:
- Be warm and appreciative, but give a clear, unmistakable no — no 'maybe later' that invites a follow-up I don't want.
- Keep the reason short and honest; do NOT over-explain or list excuses that sound defensive.
- If I have an alternative, offer it; if not, don't manufacture one.
- End on a genuinely friendly note that keeps the door open for the relationship, not the request.
- Under 110 words. No apologizing three times.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) THE EMAIL
(b) FIRMER VERSION for when they push back
(c) THE ONE SENTENCE TO CUT if I want to sound less guilty

Title: Email Tone Doctor: Will This Land the Way I Think?
Teaser: Audits your draft for how it'll actually read — too harsh, too soft, passive-aggressive, or unclear — then fixes it before you hit send.
PromptHint: Paste the [DRAFT], say who it's [TO] and the [IMPRESSION YOU WANT] to leave (e.g. confident, warm).
Prompt:
You are a communication analyst who predicts how an email will be received. Stress-test my draft before I send it.

WHO IT'S TO: [TO]
IMPRESSION I WANT TO LEAVE: [IMPRESSION YOU WANT]
MY DRAFT:
[DRAFT]

Be candid, not flattering.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. TONE READ — how this likely lands on a first read; rate confidence / warmth / clarity 1-10 each, plus the gut reaction it may trigger
2. RISKY LINES — quote any sentence that could read as passive-aggressive, defensive, demanding, vague, or accidentally cold
3. MISREADS — the most likely way the reader misinterprets my intent
4. THE FIX — a rewritten version that achieves the impression I want, same length or shorter
5. HIGHEST-IMPACT CHANGE — the single word or phrase to change first

Title: Announce a Change Without Triggering Panic or Backlash
Teaser: Drafts an internal announcement — a policy shift, reorg, price change, or new tool — that gets buy-in by leading with the 'why' and pre-empting the objections.
PromptHint: Describe [THE CHANGE], the [AUDIENCE], the real [WHY], and the [BIGGEST OBJECTION] you expect.
Prompt:
You are an internal-comms lead who rolls out hard changes without sparking panic, rumors, or a comment-thread pile-on. Write the announcement email.

THE CHANGE: [THE CHANGE]
AUDIENCE (team, company, customers): [AUDIENCE]
THE REAL WHY: [WHY]
BIGGEST OBJECTION I EXPECT: [BIGGEST OBJECTION]

Think first (don't show this): what will the audience feel in the first 5 seconds, and what rumor will fill the gap if I'm vague?

Rules:
- Lead with the change AND the honest 'why' in the first 3 lines — no slow build-up, no corporate euphemisms ('synergies', 'right-sizing').
- Name what's changing, what's NOT changing, and exactly what each person should do next.
- Pre-empt the biggest objection directly instead of hoping no one notices it.
- Acknowledge the human impact where it's real; don't fake-cheerlead a hard change.
- Give a clear channel for questions. Under 200 words, scannable.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
(a) SUBJECT LINE — clear, non-alarming
(b) THE ANNOUNCEMENT EMAIL
(c) ANTICIPATED Q&A — the 3 toughest questions people will ask, with a one-line honest answer each
